macro_events.event_summary: Iran threatens to expand military conflict beyond the Middle East if the U.S. and Israel resume strikes, raising geopolitical risk premium on energy and defense.
primary_drivers: Oil supply risk premium from potential Strait of Hormuz disruption
Elevated defense spending expectations and munitions replenishment demand
Flight-to-safety bid for gold and defensive assets

beneficiaries.rationale: Largest US integrated oil major with significant upstream leverage; direct beneficiary of Brent risk premium from Iran/Hormuz threat. ~40% of global seaborne crude transits Hormuz.
High-beta US oil producer with Permian leverage; historically outperforms peers on crude spikes given operational leverage and no refining offset.
Prime contractor for THAAD, PAC-3 Patriot missiles and Javelins — exact systems being depleted/resupplied in Israel and Gulf allies. Direct munitions replenishment exposure.
Maker of Patriot interceptors, SM-3/SM-6 (used by US Navy in Red Sea/Gulf) and Tomahawks. Direct beneficiary of munitions burn-rate from any renewed Iran strikes.
B-21/B-2 bomber and missile defense systems exposure; benefits from elevated DoD supplemental funding probability if conflict re-escalates.
Aftermarket aerospace parts monopoly on legacy defense platforms; high incremental margins on any uptick in DoD flight hours/maintenance from regional surge.
Offshore subsea equipment; tighter oil market + higher long-dated crude curve supports offshore capex sentiment. Second-order but reliable on sustained crude spikes.

losers.rationale: Highest jet fuel sensitivity among US legacy carriers (~25-30% of opex); also most international exposure including Middle East/Europe routes that may require rerouting around Iranian airspace.
Significant transatlantic and India/Tel Aviv exposure; airspace closures over Iran/Iraq force costly reroutes. Jet fuel cost spike compounds margin pressure.
Pure domestic carrier but unhedged on fuel for 2026; pure jet fuel cost passthrough hit with no international premium-cabin offset.
Cruise operator with Mediterranean and Middle East itineraries; bunker fuel costs rise with crude and itinerary cancellations historically follow regional escalations.
Commodity chemicals — naphtha/ethane feedstock costs rise with crude; limited pricing power to pass through in current demand environment.
Diesel-intensive logistics and consumer wallet share squeezed by gasoline prices; historically lags on oil spikes via consumer discretionary squeeze.
High-beta growth name vulnerable to risk-off + rising long-end yields if oil spike re-stokes inflation expectations; positioning unwind risk.

headlines: Iran threatens to extend conflict ‘beyond the region’ if U.S. and Israel resume attacks

macro_events.event_summary: Putin and Xi revive the stalled Power of Siberia 2 gas pipeline deal amid an Iran war that is roiling global energy markets, redirecting Russian gas flows to China and tightening seaborne LNG/oil supply for everyone else.
primary_drivers: Iran war risk premium on Middle East oil & LNG supply (Strait of Hormuz exposure)
Russian pipeline gas re-routed to China reduces marginal seaborne LNG available to Europe/Asia spot market
Geopolitical realignment increases structural demand for US LNG export capacity and non-OPEC oil

beneficiaries.rationale: Cheniere is the largest US LNG exporter; with Russian pipe gas going to China instead of marginal LNG cargoes and Middle East LNG (Qatar) transit at risk via Hormuz, European/Asian buyers bid up US Gulf Coast LNG. Direct margin and contract-pricing benefit.
Valero is a US Gulf Coast refiner with no Iranian/Russian crude exposure; benefits from wider crack spreads as Brent rallies on Iran war risk while it can source WTI/Canadian/Latam crude.
Diamondback is a pure-play Permian E&P with high oil beta and no international exposure; direct beneficiary of Brent/WTI risk premium from Iran conflict.
Largest US natural gas pure-play; Henry Hub strengthens as US LNG export demand rises on tighter global gas market from Russia-China pipeline diverting volumes away from spot LNG.
Targa operates Permian gas gathering/processing and NGL export infrastructure at Mont Belvieu; benefits from higher US gas/NGL pricing and export volumes as global buyers turn to US.
Integrated major with large upstream oil leverage to Brent plus growing LNG portfolio (Golden Pass coming online); double exposure to both transmission mechanisms.

losers.rationale: Delta has the highest jet fuel cost sensitivity among US legacy carriers and limited fuel hedging; Brent spike from Iran war directly compresses margins, with no offsetting benefit.
Southwest's hedging program has been wound down; domestic-heavy network means it can't pass through fuel costs as easily as international carriers. Direct margin hit from oil spike.
Dow's US ethylene crackers use natural gas/ethane feedstock — rising Henry Hub from LNG pull plus oil-linked global chemicals competition squeeze both ends. Also direct European gas exposure.
Caterpillar has meaningful China construction/mining equipment revenue; deepening Russia-China axis raises secondary sanctions risk and signals further US-China decoupling on dual-use goods.
Tesla Shanghai is ~40% of global production/sales; tighter US-China geopolitical tension from Russia-China energy axis increases tail risk of export controls or retaliation.

headlines: Putin-Xi talks revive stalled Russian gas pipeline as Iran war rattles energy markets

macro_events.event_summary: EU cleared a major procedural hurdle toward finalizing a U.S. trade pact, allowing it to avoid Trump's threatened tariff hikes, particularly on autos.
primary_drivers: Removal of auto tariff overhang on European exporters to US
Reduced input cost risk for US importers of EU goods
Improved transatlantic trade flow certainty lifts industrials/logistics

beneficiaries.rationale: Stellantis manufactures key models in Italy/France (Maserati, Fiat 500e, some Jeep variants) and ships to US; avoidance of Trump auto tariff hike directly preserves US-market margin.
Diageo's Scotch/Irish whiskey exports to US were caught in prior EU-US tariff disputes; a comprehensive deal lowers re-tariff risk on spirits category.

losers.rationale: Ford is most US-domestic of Detroit 3; loses the relative competitive tailwind it would have gotten if EU rivals (Mercedes, BMW, VW) faced tariff walls in the US market.
Same logic as Ford — GM's largely US/Mexico production base would have benefited from EU peers being tariffed; deal removes that potential moat.
Tesla's US-built fleet would have gained relative pricing advantage vs tariffed European EVs (Porsche Taycan, Audi e-tron, Mercedes EQ); deal neutralizes that edge.

headlines: EU clears major hurdle to finalize U.S. trade pact — and sidestep Trump tariff hikes

macro_events.event_summary: UK April CPI eased to 2.8%, but the BoE and analysts expect the disinflation to be temporary, limiting the scope for aggressive rate cuts.
primary_drivers: Modest GBP/BoE rate path repricing — softer near-term print but sticky outlook caps the move
Limited direct US equity transmission since it's a UK domestic CPI release, not a global inflation shock
headlines: UK inflation rate eases to 2.8% in April, but slowdown is expected to be short-lived
